model_findings.description: The new `serve --openai` command advertises local-only tooling but accepts arbitrary `--host` values and passes them directly to `uvicorn.run` with no authentication or loopback validation. A user or wrapper can bind this proxy to `0.0.0.0`, exposing local agent CLIs and the user's Hermes/Codex/Gemini/Claude credentials/config to any network client that can reach the port. For a proxy that executes paid/local agent commands from request bodies, non-loopback binds should be rejected unless an explicit authenticated remote mode is added.

Several backends pass the flattened user prompt as a command-line argument (`--prompt=...`, `-- ...`, or `--oneshot=...`) instead of stdin or a protected temp file. Chat prompts often contain source code, credentials, or private user data, and command-line arguments are observable via process listings by other local processes/users while the backend runs; very large prompts can also hit OS argument-length limits before the backend starts. Codex already uses stdin, and the other routes should use the same pattern or another non-argv transport.

The request model allows arbitrary extra OpenAI fields, but `/v1/chat/completions` does not inspect or reject `stream: true`; it always returns a normal `chat.completion` JSON object. Many OpenAI-compatible clients switch to SSE parsing when they send `stream: true`, so silently ignoring the flag produces a protocol mismatch rather than a clear unsupported-feature error. Since Phase 1 is intentionally non-streaming, the handler should return a 400-style OpenAI error for `stream: true` until streaming is implemented.

Each chat completion request is flattened and immediately launches a backend CLI with up to a 120-second timeout, but there is no request body size limit, message count/content limit, or concurrency limiter around backend execution. A client can submit many large requests that consume memory during JSON parsing/prompt construction and tie up threadpool workers plus expensive agent subprocesses until timeout. Even for localhost tooling, this should have bounded input sizes and a semaphore/rate limit around subprocess launches.

Adding activity_schema to WRITER_CORRECTION_GATES routes this failure through the existing writer-correction path, but that path renders a module.md-only prompt and accepts module.md-only patches for every gate except strict_json_parse. The activity_schema diagnostic points at activities.yaml item fields, yet the correction prompt explicitly forbids returning or patching activities.yaml, so an auto-correction retry cannot fix the failed gate and may instead rewrite module.md uselessly until the pipeline exhausts retries. This gate needs an artifact-aware correction path, or it should be excluded from WRITER_CORRECTION_GATES until corrections can modify activities.yaml.

The test now feeds _citation_gate two synthetic Grade 1 source_ref values instead of loading the module's actual resources, so it can pass while the real my-morning resources remain stale. In the current tree curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/my-morning/resources.yaml still cites Захарійчук Grade 4 p.162/p.163, which no longer matches the updated plan references; this patch therefore weakens the regression test rather than proving the module's citations resolve.
